segments.title: A blockade force at Montevideo
segments.summary: Force K's cruiser force should have been sufficient to contain Admiral Graf Spee if the task was merely a blockade off Montevideo.
segments.body: Clarke begins by asking why Force K was sent with such powerful reinforcements when HMS Cumberland, Achilles, and Ajax could probably sustain a blockade of the German raider. The comparison frames the central puzzle: a force suited to containment was already present, so the new arrivals imply a more demanding contingency.

evidence.note: Clarke weighs Cumberland, Achilles, and Ajax against the needs of a simple blockade.

segments.title: Renown closes the escape route
segments.summary: HMS Renown made a breakout by Admiral Graf Spee far less plausible, turning a blockade into a secure trap.
segments.body: Clarke's answer is that Renown was not simply another ship on station: her speed and heavy guns made sure the raider could not fight its way free. The battlecruiser's presence removed the escape option that a cruiser-only cordon could not dismiss as confidently.

evidence.note: Clarke says Renown would ensure the raider could not find a way out.

segments.title: Ark Royal and the Montevideo contingency
segments.summary: Sending Ark Royal and the newly reconstructed Renown suggested that Force K was preparing for a showdown beyond an ordinary blockade.
segments.body: Clarke stresses that Ark Royal was Britain's newest strike carrier and Renown had just emerged from a major reconstruction, an expensive combination to send for routine containment. He reads the deployment as preparation for Uruguay being unable to compel Graf Spee to leave, or for the ship attempting to reach Argentina. In either case, carrier aircraft gave the Royal Navy a means to finish the operation at sea or in harbour, despite the legal and diplomatic risks he notes.

evidence.note: Clarke identifies Ark Royal as the newest strike carrier and Renown as recently reconstructed.
He links the force to a possible failure of Uruguayan control and an attack in harbour or at sea.
